skill.name: azure-mgmt-botservice-dotnet
skill.description: Guides Azure Bot Service management with the Azure Resource Manager SDK for .NET, including bot resources, channels, and connection settings.
skill.summary: Manage Azure Bot Service resources and channels with the Azure Resource Manager SDK for .NET.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many Ruby backtick and weak cryptography patterns, but the evidence is markdown code fences, inline API names, and documentation text in SKILL.md. No prompt injection, malicious network behavior, credential exfiltration, or executable helper script was found. Remaining risk is low because the skill documents Azure credential environment variables and resource-changing SDK calls.

low_findings.title: Azure Credential Variables Documented
low_findings.description: The skill documents AZURE_TENANT_ID, AZURE_CLIENT_ID, and AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET for service principal authentication. This is legitimate Azure SDK guidance, but users must avoid exposing these values in prompts, logs, or shared files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable names are explicitly documented and include a client secret placeholder. The context is setup guidance, not evidence of credential collection or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Resource-Changing Azure SDK Examples
low_findings.description: The skill includes examples for creating, updating, deleting, and regenerating keys for Azure Bot Service resources. These are expected management operations, but they can affect live Azure resources if copied without review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced examples directly call Azure management-plane operations. They are normal for this skill, but their side effects are clear from the code context.

low_findings.title: External Documentation And Sample Endpoint Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are a sample bot endpoint and official reference links. No evidence found that the skill sends user data or credentials to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in documentation tables and a bot endpoint example. There is no executable network client or hidden data transfer logic in the skill file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Code Spans Misclassified As Ruby Backticks
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analysis reported many Ruby shell backtick findings, but the matching text is markdown code fences and inline C# or package names. No Ruby code execution pattern was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown blocks or inline code identifiers, not Ruby source code. The repository contains only SKILL.md, so there is no executable wrapper implementing these snippets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Is Not Present
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptography on lines containing ordinary Bot Service terms and descriptions. No MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak cryptographic implementation was found in the cited context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are descriptions, enum tables, or bot metadata examples. I did not find evidence of cryptographic code or insecure algorithm selection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Dismissed
dangerous_patterns.description: The heuristic combined command, network, and credential signals, but the file is a documentation guide. No evidence found of code that executes commands, reads credentials, and sends data over the network.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky signals are documentation snippets and links in one markdown file. There is no executable skill code or prompt text instructing covert behavior.

content.value_statement: Azure Bot Service setup often requires several management-plane SDK calls and channel-specific models. This skill gives focused .NET guidance for creating bots, configuring channels, and handling common ARM operations.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install Azure.ResourceManager.BotService and Azure.Identity packages.
Explains DefaultAzureCredential setup for Azure Resource Manager clients.
Provides examples for creating and updating Azure Bot resources.
Covers DirectLine, Teams, Web Chat, and other Bot Service channel types.
Lists key SDK classes, enum values, and related Bot Framework packages.
Includes basic RequestFailedException handling for ARM operations.
limitations: It is a documentation skill, not a runnable deployment tool.
It does not validate current Azure package versions at runtime.
It does not create Azure resources without user-provided code execution.
It does not replace Azure security review or subscription governance.

use_cases.title: Provision a Bot Resource
use_cases.description: Create a BotResource with the Azure Resource Manager SDK for .NET and choose the correct SKU and app type.

use_cases.title: Configure Bot Channels
use_cases.description: Add DirectLine, Teams, Web Chat, or other channels using the correct channel model classes.

use_cases.title: Review SDK Migration Details
use_cases.description: Map Bot Service management concepts to ARM client classes, collections, and async operations.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Bot Service Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me set up a .NET project for Azure Bot Service management. Include required packages and authentication prerequisites.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Bot Resource
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to create an Azure Bot resource with Azure.ResourceManager.BotService in .NET. Explain each required property.

prompt_templates.title: Add DirectLine and Teams
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate guidance for configuring DirectLine and Microsoft Teams channels for an existing Azure Bot resource.

prompt_templates.title: Harden Production Bot Management
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Azure Bot Service management approach for production. Focus on managed identity, secure channel settings, and error handling.

output_examples.input: User asks how to configure DirectLine for an Azure bot.
output_examples.output: The answer identifies the bot resource, gets its channel collection, creates DirectLine channel data, enables v3, and recommends secure site settings.

output_examples.input: User asks which SDK classes manage Bot Service resources.
output: BotCollection handles bot create and list operations.

output_examples.input: User asks how to handle Azure API errors.
output_examples.output: The response recommends catching RequestFailedException, checking status codes, and logging Azure error codes without exposing secrets.

best_practices: Use DefaultAzureCredential and prefer managed identity for production automation.
Review create, update, delete, and key regeneration calls before running generated code.
Keep client secrets out of prompts, logs, source control, and shared examples.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real Azure client secrets into chat prompts or generated files.
Do not run delete or key regeneration examples against production without approval.
Do not assume sample package versions are current without checking NuGet.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy a bot automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides .NET SDK guidance that a user can adapt and run in their own environment.

faq.question: Which Azure SDK package does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on Azure.ResourceManager.BotService for Azure Resource Manager management-plane operations.

faq.question: Can it configure Teams and DirectLine channels?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes examples for Teams, DirectLine, Web Chat, and other channel model types.

faq.question: Does it require Azure credentials?
faq.answer: The examples rely on Azure authentication. Users should use managed identity or secure service principal handling.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found documentation risks but no malicious code, prompt injection, or credential exfiltration.

faq.question: What should users verify before running code from it?
faq.answer: Users should verify subscription scope, resource group names, package versions, and any operation that changes live resources.
